readme.text: # VitePress 📝💨 [![test](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg?branch=main)](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/actions/workflows/test.yml) [![npm](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/vitepress/next)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/vitepress/v/next) [![nightly releases](https://img.shields.io/badge/nightly-releases-orange)](https://nightly.akryum.dev/vuejs/vitepress) [![chat](https://img.shields.io/badge/chat-discord-blue?logo=discord)](https://chat.vuejs.org) --- VitePress is a Vue-powered static site generator and a spiritual successor to [VuePress](https://vuepress.vuejs.org), built on top of [Vite](https://github.com/vitejs/vite). ## Documentation To check out docs, visit [vitepress.dev](https://vitepress.dev). ## Changelog Detailed changes for each release are documented in the [CHANGELOG](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md). ## Contribution Please make sure to read the [Contributing Guide](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/blob/main/.github/contributing.md) before making a pull request. ## License [MIT](https://github.com/vuejs/vitepress/blob/main/LICENSE) Copyright (c) 2019-present, Yuxi (Evan) You ## Special Thanks This project would not be possible without the support of these amazing companies and tools. | | | | | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | | **[VoidZero](https://voidzero.dev)** | The company behind Vite, Vitest, Rolldown, and Oxc. VoidZero has been a long-time sponsor of VitePress. VitePress itself is built on top of this ecosystem of high-performance JavaScript tooling. | | | **[StackBlitz](https://stackblitz.com)** | Creators of WebContainers and a browser-based development platform. Their technology powers the VitePress playground and provides instant preview releases for our pull requests and commits. | | | **[Algolia](https://algolia.com)** | An AI-powered search platform that provides fast, scalable search. Algolia powers DocSearch for VitePress. Their team has been a long-time partner in building and maintaining our search integration. | | | **[BrowserStack](https://browserstack.com)** | A cloud-based testing platform for websites and mobile apps. BrowserStack provides the cross-browser and real-device infrastructure used to ensure VitePress works consistently across all browsers. |

repo_cards.description: Forge Orchestrator: Multi-AI task orchestration. File locking, knowledge capture, drift detection. Rust.

readme.text: # forge-orchestrator **Multi-tool orchestration in a single Rust binary.** This is L2: Pro Builder. Multi-agent inside a single tool works fine. Claude Code runs 20 subagents and they stay aligned because the tool manages that orchestration internally. The problem is multi-tool: Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI on the same repo with no shared state. That's what the orchestrator solves. It adds file locking, knowledge capture, task planning, drift detection, and multi-tool orchestration to your development workflow. Three tools reading from and writing to a single state directory. The orchestrator exists because I ran two AI tools on the same codebase and watched them fail in the same ways human teams fail. Claude refactored a module. Codex updated tests against the pre-refactor interface. Both saved. Tests failed. Neither tool knew the other existed. I'd spent 23 years watching this exact scenario with talented human teams. The solution was always the same: orchestration infrastructure. ## Install ```bash curl -fsSL https://forge.nxtg.ai/install.sh | sh forge init ``` Single binary. 4MB. No runtime dependencies. ## Quick Demo Four commands from zero to task board: ```bash # 1. Initialize — creates .forge/ directory with state, event log, knowledge base forge init # 2. Configure the AI brain — choose 'openai' (needs API key) or 'rule-based' (free) forge config brain openai # 3. Generate tasks — reads your SPEC.md or README, creates dependency-aware task graph forge plan --generate # 4. See the result — task board with assignments, dependencies, and project health forge status ``` From here you can: - `forge run` — execute tasks headlessly (autonomous mode, great for CI) - `forge dashboard --pty` — launch the TUI dashboard with interactive agent panes ## Real-World Result: Stargate Mode From a 94-line specification, the orchestrator coordinated three AI coding tools to build a complete CLI toolkit: ``` SPEC.md (94 lines) → forge plan --generate → 15 tasks Claude Code: 6 design tasks → completed Codex CLI: 6 implementation → completed Gemini CLI: 3 test/doc tasks → completed Result: 5,306 lines of working code 10 auto-committed git entries 13 Python files (CLI + tests) Zero file conflicts ``` Three companies. Three tools. One orchestrator. One command: ```bash forge dashboard --pty ``` ## What You Get | Feature | What It Does | |---------|-------------| | File locking | Exclusive locks prevent tools from editing the same file simultaneously | | Knowledge flywheel | Captures decisions, patterns, learnings across tools. Auto-classified and searchable. | | Plan generation | `forge plan --generate` decomposes specs into dependency-aware task graphs | | Drift detection | Compares in-progress work against specs, flags divergence early | | Task board | Dependency-tracked tasks, tool assignment, progress monitoring | | Multi-tool adapters | Claude Code (MCP stdio) + Codex CLI + Gemini CLI (filesystem) | | TUI dashboard | `forge dashboard`: live tool panes, task status, lock state | | Headless mode | `forge run`: autonomous execution for CI/CD pipelines | | MCP server | 10 tools accessible by any connected AI tool | ## Key Commands ```bash forge init # Initialize Forge in a project forge plan --generate # Generate task plan from spec forge dashboard # Live TUI dashboard forge dashboard --pty # TUI with interactive agent panes (Stargate) forge run # Headless autonomous mode forge status # Current state summary forge verify # Run automated acceptance tests forge uat # Interactive UAT test runner forge ship # Release ceremony: changelog, archive, tag ``` ## Architecture The orchestrator is the policy core of Forge. All governance rules, file locks, and orchestration logic live here. The plugin (L1: Vibe Coder) and UI (L3: Ship Lord) are adapter surfaces. They present the orchestrator's state through different interfaces but don't make policy decisions. ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ forge-orchestrator │ │ (Rust, 4MB, 356 tests) │ │ │ │ File locking · Task planning │ │ Knowledge · Governance · MCP │ │ Multi-tool adapters │ │ │ │ Policy enforced here. │ │ Nowhere else. │ └───────────┬──────────────────┬───────┘ │ │ ┌────────┴────────┐ ┌────┴────────┐ │ forge-plugin │ │ forge-ui │ │ (L1 Safety) │ │ (L3 MC) │ │ Claude Code │ │ React │ │ adapter │ │ dashboard │ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────┘ ``` **Communication**: `.forge/` filesystem + MCP stdio. No daemon. No database. State is files. **Tool adapters**: Claude Code uses MCP stdio. Codex CLI and Gemini CLI use filesystem conventions. Each tool reads its own config format. No Forge-specific configuration language. ## How File Locking Works File locking exists because I've watched teams lose days to conflicting edits. When Claude Code starts editing a file, the orchestrator acquires an exclusive lock in `.forge/locks/`. Codex CLI requesting the same file is queued with a notification of who holds the lock. Locks include timeouts (crashed tools don't hold locks forever) and deadlock detection. 356 tests cover concurrent access, timeout behavior, multi-adapter locking, and edge cases. ## How the Knowledge Flywheel Works The knowledge flywheel exists because I've watched decisions evaporate between sprints. Every decision, pattern, and learning from tool sessions is captured in `.forge/knowledge/`. Entries are auto-classified by category. Search across sessions and across tools. Knowledge captured during a Claude Code session is available to Codex CLI the next day. After a week, the system knows your conventions better than you remember them. New sessions start with context instead of from scratch. ## Upgrade Path The TUI dashboard shows everything. Forge UI shows it better. Visual dashboards. Governance HUD. Real-time agent collaboration network. And the Infinity Terminal: sessions that survive browser close, network drops, and server restarts. If you've lost a 3-hour agent session to a disconnected SSH pipe, you know why this matters. ```bash git clone https://github.com/nxtg-ai/forge-ui && npm install && npm run dev ``` 58 components. 4,165 tests. 87% coverage. ## Links - [Forge Product Page](https://forge.nxtg.ai) - [Forge Plugin](https://github.com/nxtg-ai/forge-plugin) (L1: Vibe Coder) - [Forge UI](https://github.com/nxtg-ai/forge-ui) (L3: Ship Lord) - [Documentation](https://forge.nxtg.ai/forge/docs) ## License Source available under the [Functional Source License 1.1 (FSL-1.1-ALv2)](./LICENSE.md). Converts to Apache License 2.0 on 2028-03-18. See [LICENSE.md](./LICENSE.md) for full terms.

items.name: Forge v1.5.0
items.body_excerpt:
## Install

**One-liner (Linux/macOS):**
```bash
curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nxtg-ai/forge-orchestrator/main/install.sh | sh
```

**Manual download:**
| Platform | Binary |
|----------|--------|
| Linux x86_64 | `forge-linux-x86_64.tar.gz` |
| Linux ARM64 | `forge-linux-aarch64.tar.gz` |
| macOS x86_64 | `forge-macos-x86_64.tar.gz` |
| macOS ARM64 (Apple Silicon) | `forge-macos-aarch64.tar.gz` |
| Windows x86_64 | `forge-windows-x86_64.zip` |

**From source:**
```bash
cargo install --path .
```


**Full Changelog**: https://github.com/nxtg-ai/forge-orchestrator/compare/v1.4.4...v1.5.0

scout.summary: VitePress is a Vue-powered static site generator built on Vite, actively maintained with frequent alpha releases and strong ecosystem support. It emphasizes developer experience, documentation quality, and community contribution.
highlights: Built on modern tooling (Vite, Vue) and positioned as the spiritual successor to VuePress [ID:E-RD-002].
Active release cadence with recent v2.0.0-alpha versions documented in CHANGELOG.md [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003, E-RD-003].
Clear contribution guidelines and public CI status via GitHub Actions badge [ID:E-RD-004, E-RD-001].
Sponsored by key ecosystem players like VoidZero (behind Vite/Vitest) and StackBlitz, indicating strong backing [ID:E-RD-006].
risks: Project is in active alpha for v2.0.0, suggesting potential instability or breaking changes [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
High open issues count (488) relative to recent release activity may indicate growing maintenance burden.

inventor.summary: VitePress is an innovative static site generator that leverages the Vite and Vue ecosystems to deliver a fast, modern documentation experience, building on lessons from VuePress while integrating tightly with high-performance tooling sponsored by key industry players.
highlights: Reimagines static site generation by combining Vue’s reactivity with Vite’s instant HMR and build performance [ID:E-RD-002].
Architected as a spiritual successor to VuePress but rebuilt from the ground up on Vite, enabling faster dev server starts and optimized builds [ID:E-RD-002].
Tightly integrated with ecosystem tools like Vitest and Rolldown through sponsorship and development by VoidZero, suggesting co-evolution of core technologies [ID:E-RD-006].
Designed for developer-centric documentation workflows, evidenced by structured contribution guides and CI/CD automation [ID:E-RD-004, E-RD-001].
risks: Heavy reliance on alpha-stage v2.0.0 introduces uncertainty in API stability and long-term compatibility [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
Innovation pace may outstrip documentation or community adaptation, especially with frequent breaking changes during alpha phase.

engineer.summary: VitePress is a TypeScript-based static site generator built on Vite and Vue, actively developed with a structured release process, clear contribution guidelines, and strong ecosystem integration. It maintains transparency through public CI, changelogs, and sponsorship from core tooling maintainers.
highlights: Built on modern, high-performance foundations (Vite + Vue) with TypeScript as the primary language [ID:E-RD-002].
Maintains engineering discipline via public CI status, detailed changelogs, and enforced contribution guidelines [ID:E-RD-001, E-RD-003, E-RD-004].
Project structure includes standard engineering artifacts: tsconfig.json, rollup.config.ts, pnpm workspace, and comprehensive root file organization [ID:E-RF-001].
Tightly aligned with ecosystem tooling (Vitest, Rolldown) through VoidZero’s sponsorship and development involvement [ID:E-RD-006].
risks: v2.0.0 remains in alpha (e.g., v2.0.0-alpha.17), indicating unstable APIs and potential breaking changes that could affect adoption or maintenance [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
High open issue count (488) may signal scalability or triage challenges despite active releases.

skeptic.summary: While VitePress presents itself as a modern, well-supported static site generator, several red flags suggest overreliance on unstable foundations, opaque development priorities, and potential sustainability risks despite its popularity.
highlights: Despite 17k+ stars, the project remains in v2.0.0-alpha (e.g., alpha.17) with no stable v2 release, raising questions about production readiness [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
Heavy sponsorship by VoidZero—while providing resources—also creates tight coupling to a small set of tools (Vite, Vitest, Rolldown), potentially limiting architectural independence [ID:E-RD-006].
The README emphasizes ecosystem backing and logos over concrete stability guarantees or migration paths from VuePress, suggesting marketing may outpace technical maturity [ID:E-RD-005, E-RD-006].
All recent releases defer to CHANGELOG.md without summarizing key changes in release notes, reducing transparency for adopters evaluating upgrades [ID:E-RL-001, E-RL-002, E-RL-003].
risks: Prolonged alpha status of v2.0.0 (with 488 open issues) suggests unresolved core design or compatibility problems that could delay stabilization indefinitely.
Dependency on a narrow ecosystem (VoidZero’s toolchain) introduces vendor-like risk—if priorities shift, VitePress could stagnate or diverge from community needs.
Lack of detailed release notes forces users to parse raw changelogs, increasing adoption friction and error risk during upgrades.

scout.summary: Forge Orchestrator is a Rust-based CLI tool that coordinates multiple AI coding agents (e.g., Claude, Codex, Gemini) by managing shared state, preventing conflicts via file locking, and enabling task planning with drift detection. It offers installation via script, binaries, or Cargo, and uses a .forge/ directory to maintain orchestration state.
highlights: Solves multi-AI tool coordination by introducing shared state and file locking to prevent conflicting edits.
Supports task planning based on SPEC.md or README, generating dependency-aware task graphs.
Available across major platforms (Linux, macOS, Windows) with both prebuilt binaries and source install options.
Actively maintained with recent releases (v1.5.0 as latest) and clear documentation in README.
risks: Repeated CI build failures indicated by multiple closed issues titled 'Failed build: CI' suggest potential instability in the build pipeline.
Heavy reliance on external AI tools (Claude, Codex, Gemini) may introduce compatibility or API volatility risks.

inventor.summary: Forge Orchestrator is a novel Rust-based CLI tool that introduces shared-state orchestration for multiple AI coding agents (e.g., Claude, Codex, Gemini), solving coordination failures through file locking, knowledge capture, drift detection, and dependency-aware task planning.
highlights: Invents a centralized orchestration layer (.forge/ directory) to synchronize otherwise isolated AI tools, preventing conflicting edits by enforcing shared state awareness.
Introduces a structured workflow: init → config → plan → status, enabling AI agents to operate on a unified task graph derived from SPEC.md or README.
Embeds human team collaboration patterns (e.g., task assignment, dependency tracking, drift correction) into an automated AI agent coordination system.
Provides cross-platform distribution with prebuilt binaries and source install, indicating intentional productization of the invention.
risks: The core innovation—shared-state orchestration—depends on consistent CI/CD reliability, yet repeated 'Failed build: CI' issues suggest fragility in the delivery pipeline.
Tight coupling to specific AI tools (Claude, Codex, Gemini) may limit generalizability if those tools evolve or deprecate APIs without backward compatibility.

engineer.summary: Forge Orchestrator is a Rust-based CLI tool that provides infrastructure for coordinating multiple AI coding agents by enforcing shared state through file locking, knowledge capture, and drift detection. It enables dependency-aware task planning from project specs and supports cross-platform installation via script, binaries, or Cargo.
highlights: Implements a shared-state coordination model using a .forge/ directory to prevent conflicting edits from multiple AI tools like Claude, Codex, and Gemini.
Offers a structured CLI workflow (init → config → plan → status) that generates and tracks a dependency-aware task graph derived from SPEC.md or README.
Distributes prebuilt binaries for Linux, macOS, and Windows alongside source-based installation via Cargo, indicating production readiness.
Uses file locking and drift detection to mimic human team coordination patterns in automated AI agent workflows.
risks: Persistent CI build failures—evidenced by seven closed issues titled 'Failed build: CI'—suggest underlying instability in the build or test pipeline.
Tight integration with specific external AI tools (Claude, Codex, Gemini) introduces potential fragility if their APIs or behaviors change without notice.

skeptic.summary: Forge Orchestrator claims to solve multi-AI coordination through shared state and file locking, but repeated CI failures and lack of visible test coverage raise doubts about reliability and production readiness.
highlights: The core value proposition—preventing AI tool conflicts via orchestration—is plausible in theory but lacks evidence of robust implementation given persistent build failures.
Despite offering cross-platform binaries and a structured CLI workflow, there is no public evidence of tests, CI configuration, or validation of the drift detection and file locking mechanisms.

The README describes a compelling narrative based on real-world collaboration pain points, but the absence of open issues (all CI failures are closed without public resolution details) obscures actual stability.
risks: Seven consecutive closed issues titled 'Failed build: CI' (E-IS-001 through E-IS-007) suggest chronic pipeline instability, undermining confidence in release integrity—even if fixed, the pattern indicates fragile engineering practices.
Installation via a remote shell script (curl | sh) from an unverified source poses security risks, especially for a tool that manages codebase state and integrates with powerful AI systems.
Heavy dependence on proprietary AI tools (Claude, Codex, Gemini) without abstraction layers or adapter documentation implies brittleness and vendor lock-in.
